one_line_takeaway: SIGIL proposes a statistically principled (Neyman-Pearson FPR-controlled) proactive canary-watermarking scheme for proving LLM training-data membership, but every reported AUC/detection-rate number (overall AUC 0.892, per-strategy AUC 0.875-0.903, paraphrase-robust AUC 0.864) comes from a hand-calibrated 36,000-trial Monte Carlo simulation rather than any real trained model.
key_finding: Under the authors' own calibrated simulator (not on real trained models), overall trial-level AUC = 0.892 across all 36,000 trials, rising monotonically with injection rate from AUC 0.831 at r=0.1% to AUC 0.947 at r=10%. Trained-model MIS is centered at 1.795 +/- 1.045 versus untrained MIS centered at 0.000 +/- 1.003, consistent with the assumed N(0,1) null. Per-strategy simulated AUC: Code Pattern 0.903 (Cohen's d=1.84, highest imperceptibility I=0.932), Canary Phrase 0.900, Rare Token 0.900, Semantic Topic Steering 0.883, Syntactic Structure 0.875 (Cohen's d=1.63, lowest). All four simulated factors (injection rate F=504.14, model size F=62.25, canary strategy F=33.38, mixture ratio F=79.85) show significant independent effects, all p<0.001. Under simulated 100% paraphrasing (modeled survival rate 31%), AUC remains 0.864, attributed to 'semantic leakage' from topic-steering canaries surviving surface rewording; under simulated near-dedup (MinHash, survival 73%) AUC=0.891, heuristic cleaning (survival 78%) AUC=0.903. At alpha=0.01 (MIS threshold 2.326) with 10% injection on the large (7B-scale) simulated condition, detection rate is 73.4%, which the authors frame as a likelihood ratio of ~73:1 in favor of training-data membership, exceeding a commonly cited Bayes-factor 'strong evidence' threshold of 10.
core_contribution: SIGIL is a proactive, content-owner-side framework for proving that a specific document was used in LLM training. Before public release, the content owner embeds imperceptible 'canary' sequences into a document using one of five strategies (Rare Token Insertion, Canary Phrase Injection, Syntactic Structure Watermark, Semantic Topic Steering, Code Pattern Beacon). Later, given black-box query access to a suspect model, the owner computes a Membership Inference Score (MIS) — a Neyman-Pearson-style statistic MIS(M) = Phi^-1(1 - p_Welch), derived from a one-sided Welch t-test comparing log-probabilities on canary probes versus matched controls — which has formally controlled false-positive rate under the null (MIS ~ N(0,1) when the model was not trained on the watermarked data). The paper also defines a Canary Imperceptibility measure I(d,d') (edit distance combined with estimated human-detectability) and a per-strategy 'Detection Advantage' parameter calibrated from prior empirical membership-inference literature. The core empirical contribution is a large factorial Monte Carlo simulation (5 strategies x 3 model-size classes x 5 injection rates x 4 mixture ratios x 60 trials x 2 labels = 36,000 trials) that exercises this statistical framework and characterizes how AUC/detection rate vary with injection rate, model size, canary strategy, mixture ratio, and simulated adversarial defenses (paraphrasing, deduplication, heuristic cleaning).
threat_model: Forensic/IP-protection setting, not an attack paper against a live system. Defender = content owner who controls a document corpus before release and wants proof-quality evidence that an LLM was trained on it without authorization (aimed at copyright litigation / court admissibility). Adversary = the model trainer/operator, who may apply defenses to remove or dilute the signal: exact deduplication, near-deduplication (MinHash), heuristic data cleaning, and paraphrasing at 10%/50%/100% strength. The paper explicitly frames an 'adaptive adversary' who knows the specific canary strategy and could target it for removal, and proposes multi-strategy deployment as a partial mitigation (not evaluated). Detection requires black-box log-probability query access to the suspect model; the paper flags generation-only access (no log-probs) as an open extension.
limitations: The paper's own §8.4 states the central limitation plainly: all results come from a calibrated simulator, not actual LLM training runs — no real model (GPT-2, LLaMA, Mistral, etc.) was trained on watermarked data or queried; validating on real models is listed as the primary direction for future work. The simulator's parameters (null variance, delta formula coefficients, per-strategy detection advantages) are themselves calibrated to match ranges reported in prior empirical membership-inference papers (Carlini et al. 2022, Shi et al. 2024, Biderman et al. 2023 for the Pythia scaling trend), so the 36,000-trial results demonstrate internal consistency of the statistical framework and simulator, not a validated real-world detection capability. Detection also assumes black-box log-probability access to the suspect model, which not all deployed APIs provide; extension to generation-only access is left open. The paper acknowledges an adaptive adversary who knows the canary strategy could attempt targeted removal, and proposes but does not evaluate multi-strategy deployment as a countermeasure. Detection rates in the simulation are modest even in favorable conditions (33%-78% across the full grid), meaning many truly watermarked-and-trained models would go undetected at the chosen alpha even setting aside sim-to-real gap.

datasets_used: None real — fully simulated. No document corpus was actually canary-injected, no LLM was actually trained or queried; 'small/medium/large' model conditions are Pythia-scale-derived size factors (f=0.55/0.75/0.92 for 125M/1.3B/7B) plugged into a hand-specified formula, not real Pythia models run in the experiment.
quantitative_results: Simulated AUC rises from 0.831 at injection rate r=0.1% to 0.947 at r=10%
Simulated detection rate range across the 5x3 rate/model-size grid: 32.8% to 77.8% (Table 2)
Simulated per-strategy AUC: Code Pattern 0.903 (d=1.84), Canary Phrase 0.900, Rare Token 0.900, Semantic Topic 0.883, Syntactic Structure 0.875 (d=1.63)
Simulated trained-model MIS = 1.795 +/- 1.045 vs untrained MIS = 0.000 +/- 1.003
Simulated AUC under 100% paraphrasing (survival rate 31%) = 0.864; under near-dedup/MinHash (survival 73%) = 0.891; under heuristic cleaning (survival 78%) = 0.903 (Table 3)
ANOVA effects (all simulated, p<0.001): injection rate F=504.14; model size F=62.25; canary strategy F=33.38; mixture ratio F=79.85
At alpha=0.01, 10% injection, large (7B-scale) condition: simulated detection rate 73.4%, framed as ~73:1 likelihood ratio for training-data membership
baselines_compared: None run head-to-head. SIGIL performs no experimental comparison against a competing membership-inference or watermarking method under matched conditions. Numbers from prior work (Carlini et al. 2022 likelihood-ratio attack AUC up to 0.72 on GPT-2 via zlib reference; Shi et al. 2024 Min-k% Prob AUC 0.70-0.84 on LLaMA) are used only as calibration targets/references to set the simulator's null-distribution variance and detection-advantage parameters — they are not baselines SIGIL's simulated numbers were benchmarked against on shared data.
